text: This video illustrates a robot-assisted central hepatectomy indicated for centrally located hepatocellular carcinoma. The patient was a seventy-year-old male diagnosed with a hepatic mass developed in the right lobe of the liver. On this abdominal MRI, you can notice the direct invasion of the middle hepatic vein by the tumour mass, which is developed at the expense of segments four, five and eight.
The patient is positioned supine with a slight anti-Trendelenburg tilt. Four 8mm robotic trocars are inserted, along with two additional ports for table-side assistance.

The surgeon uses two bipolar forceps and an ultrasonic dissector to divide the round ligament of the liver. The falciform and anterior coronary ligaments are opened, while the dome of the liver is gently retracted downwards using the bipolar forceps from arm number one. The gallbladder is dissected from the liver bed.
A vessel loop is placed around the hepato-duodenal ligament, preparing for a potential Pringle maneuver during the parenchymal transection.

The surgeon employs the glissonian approach for elective pedicle control, detaching the hilar plate from the quadrate lobe. The right anterior glissonian pedicle is controlled without requiring liver dissection.

A small parenchymal incision is made medially above the hilar plate. The surgeon dissects the cystic plate, revealing the right anterior glissonian pedicle behind it. A laparoscopic bulldog clamp is applied to the dissected pedicle for temporary inflow occlusion.

The surgeon performs intraoperative Doppler ultrasonography to confirm the effectiveness of anterior sector clamping, with the image projected on the console. The intersectoral plane is delineated along the discoloration margin after temporarily clamping the right anterior septopedicle.

The surgeon delineates the medial plane between the left lobe and segment four, starting from the round ligament and extending to the origin of the middle hepatic vein. Transection is performed using a caudal approach, following the path of the falciform ligament.

The surgeon dissects and encircles the segment four glissonian pedicle with a vessel loop, dividing it between a haemlock clip. The right anterior sector glissonian pedicle is dissected, controlled, and divided using a vascular stapler. The parenchymal transection along the medial plane is completed, and the origin of the middle hepatic vein is encircled, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon identifies the origin of the right hepatic vein and uses a caudal approach to transect the right intersectorial plane. The transection follows the delineated demarcation line on the surface and continues along the trajectory of the right hepatic vein as the dissection proceeds deeper.
The surgeon uses a cotton tape to perform a hanging maneuver, defining the posterior section plane and improving exposure for easier transection of the remaining posterior margin.
The surgeon extracts the specimen using an endobag through a midline incision. The surgical field is then inspected to ensure hemostasis and biliostasis following the completion of the hepatectomy.
The surgeon identifies a bile leak from the right anterior glissonian pedicle and controls it using interrupted non-resorbable sutures, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
A pathological examination confirmed the diagnosis of a sixty millimetre hepatocellular carcinoma. The post-operative course was uneventful.
The abdominal drain was removed on day five after a CT scan and the patient was discharged on day eight.
